what_it_does: A self-contained acceptance test script for a local module named check.py. It supplies two small HTML screen fixtures, calls check.find_literals and check.candidate_tokens, and asserts that hardcoded literals are extracted, counted across the corpus, matched to existing token values, or flagged as candidate new tokens.
observations: Imports check via sys.path so the sibling module is discoverable.
Defines a single run() function that holds all test logic and fixtures.
Uses plain assert statements rather than a test framework.
Hardcodes two HTML strings in a screens dictionary.
Hardcodes a token_values dictionary mapping token names to resolved values.
Asserts both occurrence counting and token matching for repeated literals.
Expects #abcdef to have no matching token, making it a new-token candidate.
standouts: Acts as a RED→GREEN acceptance test before check.py is implemented.
Tests the boundary between existing-token reuse and new-token candidacy.
Counts literal occurrences across multiple files/screens.
conventions: Top-level module docstring explaining intent and run command.
Local import via sys.path.insert for sibling module.
Function-level entry point run() for direct execution.
Assertion messages include the failing value or reason.
concerns: Depends on an unshown check.py API (find_literals, candidate_tokens).
No test framework means no structured reporting or isolation.
Fixtures and token values are hardcoded, limiting coverage.
Will fail at import time if check.py does not exist.
notes: This file is the acceptance-test contract that tells the author exactly what check.py must do to turn RED→GREEN.
questions: What are the exact return types and shapes of check.find_literals and check.candidate_tokens?
How are duplicate literals normalized before counting?
Does the candidate_tokens function accept arbitrary screen dictionaries or a specific corpus format?
What token-name resolution strategy is used when multiple tokens share the same value?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the stated purpose is to test literal extraction, counting, and token matching, and that is exactly what the assertions verify.
intention_signals: It is trying to define the expected behavior of a design-token coherence checker so that an implementation can be written against concrete examples.
novelty: Treats hardcoded mockup literals as first-class token candidates with occurrence counts and explicit existing-token vs new-token classification.
contribution: A concrete acceptance test with fixtures and assertions that specify the required API behavior for literal extraction and token matching in a design-system audit tool.
summary_for_embedding: A self-contained RED→GREEN acceptance test for check.py that extracts hardcoded literals from HTML mockups, counts them across a corpus, and asserts they either match existing token values or are flagged as candidate new tokens.
intention_for_embedding: To define the expected behavior of a design-token coherence checker before implementing it.

symbols.does: Executes acceptance assertions for check.py's literal extraction and token-candidate logic.
symbols.description: Sets up two HTML screen fixtures and a token-values map, calls check.find_literals and check.candidate_tokens, asserts that repeated literals are counted across the corpus, matched to existing token values, and unmatched literals are flagged as new-token candidates, then prints a PASS message.
